speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Anna Gleason Utt was born in 1906 in Spangle. Her mother's parents, the Fricks, were one of the founders of that town and ran the hotel. On this tape, Anna Utt describes the Spangle Hotel, the move to Harvard, and life at home. Different events from her childhood and the work day of her parents are recalled.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It was a big one. I don't know how many stories it was. But Grandpa and Grandma were running as long as they had enough girls. And Grandpa lived. I don't think... Yes, she had to have it quite a while after he was gone. Because he died when my oldest brother was a baby. So that would have to be in 19... 2... 19... 1 or 19... 2. Grandpa was still running when I was a big kid, 12 years old. And of course, Mama worked in there all the time. She was at home. Quite a while afterwards because my folks lived there then until 19... 11. Guess that's when we came out here.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I have one or two platters that they used in that hotel. You know, breakfast platters. Big, heavy, thick things. Imagine girls carrying them around.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, in one big, big room.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, as one would marry off, then another one would probably help out.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And... I don't know. They didn't have the hotel when the youngest girl was growing up.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She's dead now too. They're all gone except Mama. He was quite a big family.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: There were 12, right?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, Mama had 12. I just don't have them.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Seems like it was 10. But I can't find them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father live in Spangle?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, his parents lived in Spokane at the time, I think. Because my Grandma Gleason was a Gleason in Spokane. And my Grandma Gleason died in 1904.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And he lived up there all that time. But my Dad, at that time, was working on farms, I think. Just here and there. And he let people in Spangle go into the hotel and eat.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: He had the best apple tree there ever was on that. Next to that. And I never found it since. Of course, I wouldn't know it if I did, but it seems like it. It was so delicious that if I ever could find them, I didn't know what was inside the taste of them. It wasn't a big apple, but it was just... You bite into it, the juices run down your chin. I don't know what. It was a summer apple. And they didn't pick them. They just let the kids run to them, you know. And that was some apple tree. And then back of the hotel was their home. It's gone too. But where the highway is going up through Spangle, Spokane, that highway is right on top of it. Where the hotel would have been if the town was still. You know, the high town was more of a curve there then, you know, and the road was down a little bit. But they, uh, they must have gone down. Mama tells me about the big shade trees. Oh, they were huge. Out in front of the hotel, I remember them. And one time, I think she used to call those the salesmen. Come here and sell stuff. But stay at the hotel, you know. One day she was out there and he was picking those long ants off the tree, you know, and pulling the heads off of them and eating their eggs. And he just, um, just loved them. But, you know, even though she was grown, she still shed her wings. And that man eating ants was a big ants. It's a little hard to throw the head away with the rest.

words: That's a delicacy now, chocolate covered ants. I know it is.

speaker: Anna Utt

speaker: Anna Utt

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your parents stay in Spain?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, they stayed there until 1911. Then we can load it up like they had. They had a farm, but then they had a farm sale. And I've got that sale bill yet. And they sold out and loaded up kids. They had to have a stall or two, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they run the hotel when they stayed in Spain?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, no.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, Dad carried mail for years. And then he, you know, one horse buggy and all. And then he worked in the warehouse. The house that we lived in was up here and the warehouse was just down below just a little ways. He'd be running from dinner and lunch and stuff. And then he worked there a while. And then they got on a farm and they lived on that until he sold it to come out here. But up that creek was all friends from Spain. I guess that's why we followed him out.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: We got here in November, lived in a chicken house. It was on a place down below us, a quarter, no, three -fourths of a mile down. And it was pretty big, full of shit, you know. And then by the next winter, my dad had the log cabin built.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he build it himself?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, he did most of the work, you know. He had to have, he had someone help, I think, with raising the rafters or something like that. Otherwise... I love that old house. It wasn't much of a...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was it like, the old house?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think I have a picture I can show you pretty quick.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I got a book like this I kept under. That was my grandma's house. I mean, grandma's break, the one around the hotel.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That's that one's from Have a Birthday Sunday.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: There's one side of it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It's another side of it. Later, I think they built a cellar on out from here for vegetables and fruit and stuff.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did it have, did the upstairs, did you live in it too?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that where you neatly slept?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, that's where all of us kids slept. It was nice. That window on this end is where the coyotes came right up around the house, you know, and they get up and watch them out on moonlight nights, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They come up very close?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they come right up to the, just right up there.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Most of these pictures are long, you know, and...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That's the oldest boy. He retired a long time ago, of course. That was when he was a banker and a health care worker. And then he left his Spokane bank and he retired.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How big a house was that that they built? How big was it on the inside?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It had three rooms in it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It had a big living room and not so big bedroom, but the bedroom was a long one, but not any wider than the beds, much wider, you know, wide enough to get around. And they had three beds in that downstairs and the rest of the beds were upstairs. And then the kitchen was about like that too. A little bit bigger, you know, we had to have a big table. And then they had a bench on one side of the table. Dad always called that the crater roll because all the kids started low and lined up, you know, like that. He'd sit on this end and Mama sat next to him there. And the rest of us was lined up. And it was beautiful. I mean, you know, we had so much fun. And now we never see each other. This summer I saw two of the boys. Oh, man, that was nice. One of them is never, the other one has been in Alaska until he retired. He moved out of Montana and I had to see both of those. And just my sister here. And we don't see each other very often. We talk on the phone. But that's the funniest family.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And we just don't get together. And I would like to see them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You were all pretty close with them?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: We just always had a family reunion, you know, on something, once a year. Sometimes it was Thanksgiving, but that really was too late, you know, for some of them to come. Sometimes earlier, sometimes we'd go up to Grizzled Camp, have a reunion up there. You know, it was just so much fun.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember what it was like living in that chicken coop that first winter? Is that pretty hard?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Because it must not have been too insulated.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't remember too much. I remember since we didn't have too much furniture yet, you know, and everything, we weren't too crowded. But they had a tall box. It was taller than that. And the longer... I imagine it was about as wide. And on that wall, they hung their coats. And the little kids, you know, would be back over there and so on, you know. I remember some of the school kids came up to... The schoolhouse was just here, we were here. And some of the girls came up and asked me to come out. But at recess time, I hadn't started school yet. And so I climbed up on that box to get my coat, because I was showing off more than anything else. And then I jumped off. And it killed me, so I remember doing that. Just as plain as... The silly things you do, you always remember.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I remember... We didn't, you know, go to the neighbors and stay all night. My kids do not age too much. We could play a little bit in the daytime, but we always had to be home at night. I don't think I ever stayed all night with one of my friends. That was just something that I didn't believe in, I guess. You want to leave the kids home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you usually be home by dinner time? Or would you eat dinner at a friend's house sometimes?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, it's a new meal, but in the evening we had to be home. We could lunch with someone, you know. And he wanted his lunch there. And he used to worry when we got old enough to go out. He didn't care for that too much.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But... It was a good time we had at home.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But Ruby now, I see her. I hope.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: What did your family do anything for...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would you do when you were all together in the evenings?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: If it was good weather, of course, we were out. I didn't go too much because, you know, I was the oldest girl and Mama needed help so much.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But enough of them to play games, get up to baseball, match, or play run, cheap run. Just enough of them to do things like that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was your father playing with them too?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, he always played ball with us.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But baseball... In the wintertime, we crocheted or he would read to us nights a lot. Before electricity, he'd get back up to the little lamps we had, kerosene lamps. And he could read, you know, everybody. Every man would get to those evenings. Especially when a... I guess a side of a farmer would come in. He even kept all the stuff in there. All the stories and stuff in there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How often did that come?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think just once a month, I think. But maybe it was more. And I don't know. I just don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of stories would they be?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, I remember one of them. We just thought he could read that so well. It was one of Zane Gray's stories.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Man of the Forest, I think.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Any story that come out. And then we took a spokesman or a viewer or something. Chronicle, I think it was. And twice a week.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would he read you the paper?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: If it was good news, you know. I mean, he was up in the news that way.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would he read you the stories over and over again? If you read out to anyone?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, if we put up too big a racket, he would.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But he liked to read and he liked to listen.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: People can get by and do pressure for hard times than anything else. He always had enough to eat.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Nowadays it's different.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Kids have to be entertained away from home. And that's sad. I guess. Because kids not age. I have some greatest grandkids. But they grew up so fast.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What do you mean by that? That they grew up fast?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, it seemed like the oldest one, he's 20, he's married now. Liz and Clarkston. And the second one goes to Lewis and Clark and Lewis. College down there. And the third one now is a senior. In potlice. It just seems like we haven't got time for him to grow up that fast. Once a sophomore and yet he's a freshman. And you know they're so busy. School activities just keep them so busy.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you think that when you're growing up you have more time to play and fool around with kids too?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, things were so different. You know, like in all the days we used to have recesses. 15 minute recesses. And you'd get out and play. And then the game would continue somewhere it left off. You know, the next recess we'd have an hour noon. Well, not always. Not every winter you'd have an hour noon. But you know, it seemed like there was so much time to play. Now they don't have that in school.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: They have the sports and lots of things and all the kids occupied.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And I guess it's better. They seem to be smarter than we were by far. I think it's better to play football.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you pretty close to the sisters and brothers in your family that were about your age? Did you have good friends outside of your family?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, yeah, I had some friends alright that we chugged with. Only at school. When school was out then that was it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I had some very good friends. I had one living in California. She couldn't get up this summer though. Donna stopped. And I think I got a nice summer. When she passed I guess it was summer. And I saw her sister. Yes, you are. She was here for the first time. I guess 40 years. Maybe not that long. And maybe yes it is.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: You can't expect so much when you get over it.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: They drive them to be home, you know, like those fabulous dodge bombs. Big rugs and complete home. When they come up they drive back. That's what General Zendover Overdrive, his sister.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She named Dennis. They started in on just making a little bit of wood. And they hauled it out and sold it. Then they made more and hauled it out and sold it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They had a small sauna?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No. They just get cordoned. A wood vermin they could pick it up. Maybe on a toaster. And then they got such a big business they needed two trucks. And they kept on going and kept on going until they had a fleet of trucks. And hauling everything for everybody. They made all that good. The boys still in the business. That's what they did to make that money.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What would breakfast be like?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The chicken just got a good... In my family, Dad got up first. He built the fire, he got it warm.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When would he get up? How early? Do you know?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, then I suppose it was...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, it must have been 5 .30. Maybe earlier. And he made the coffee and cooked the oatmeal. And he always cooked the oatmeal. The kids always ate it. And I can just stay on my oatmeal. For a little bit.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then all the rest of you would get up. And then he'd yell. And we'd better get up. Let us up, you know. It wouldn't take long to... Whatever it was we had, you know. And get ready for school and be gone.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have any chores there on the house you had to do before you left in the morning? Where did you go to the house?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't think so. Just help me. I have to help the others get ready to go. But I can't remember waiting to wash dishes or anything like that. I suppose Mama does those all after.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you come home for lunch?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No. But that's the job we had to do at 6 o 'clock. That was quite a job, too. For 12 kids. Yeah, you know. We always... Mama baked bread every day.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: So we always had fresh bread.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How many loaves would she bake?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She baked...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think it was five. I think in the big pan, she had three loaves in the big pan. And two loaves in a smaller pan. That's all her oven would hold. And that's what she baked every day. And if she ran out, she would bake biscuits. And her roof would separate. And save the bread for lunches the next day. Because she got caught up in that. But most often, she would just bake bread. Every day. Every day.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have much white flour that you used? Did you have much white flour? Or was it all whole wheat that she used?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, no. We had lots of white flour. You know, you take your wheat de puce and have it ground up. Bring it back home for the winter. Then they get five gallons of honey.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They get five gallons?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And the whole model would come way up in the hundreds. She'd come 150 corn bollaries to bake. And she'd come everything.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: They had their own meat, you know, after we got started. And they were good.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have a cow for milk? And chickens?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes. No, I mean, not so many. Well, they were milking too many when they got older. But they just had three or four. Butter and milk and cottage cheese.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: They had more beef than when I got older. It was easier for Dad to butcher a pewter.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Nice and sneaky, but you'd need to run the knife into his finger under the nut. More and more. More and more. And he suffered with that bone and then walked the floor and everything else. Already decided to have his finger taken off.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did you preserve the pig? Did you smoke most of it?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah. And we had a smokehouse, you know. We'd smoke in there with the fire bone. That usually was John's job.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long would it smoke before it was smoked?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I can't remember.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you have any idea how long?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It seemed like forever, you know, when you're a kid. But I suppose now it would be like everything else. When you're a kid the hills are steeper and the trees are bigger and thicker. The snow is deeper.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And when you get old it just... We're...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Had much of the timber...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: This is my son.

words: What's your name?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Laura. Laura. Laura Schreger.

words: You?

words: Yeah, exactly. She's putting things down there I say. Uh oh. Uh oh. Profanity and everything. No, I don't think I've said anything like that, but I may have.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How you been? A little bit extra to go, how have you been?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where did you work for?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I worked for Elsie first and then... I was the store manager when I was in the alley. And...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you work in the store?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, just a couple of days when they were having a sale or something like that I did. But I just... His house, you know, worked for his wife and took care of the kids. I earned my board that way. Washes were their names.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your mother have a routine for what things she did on a certain day? I mean, would she always wash on Monday? Did she have a routine like that to you?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, she had to wash two times a week. I remember she washed on Saturday. And I suppose the other day was Tuesday or something like that. Because after Sunday she needed to clean up, you know, and do more business for the week's school lunches and stuff.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was Sunday a day of rest for you?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you work much on Sunday?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Not too often much. We always spent the morning going to Sunday school. I don't think we ever missed that. But then we'd go home to our Sunday dinner. And no, that wasn't much of a work day. It was for mama because she darned and sewed and patched and stuff like that while she was just sitting in the corner. I do those things, you know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And that was the company.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did most kids go to Sunday school?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah. I can't remember John and Virgil. But I went and little kids went. I don't know how little. They quit.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did kids from the families around you help most of them?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, I think so. If I had my own book like this I could show you some of the Sunday school classes. One time Mrs. Moore had a Sunday school class. I just don't know how many was in that, but there was a whole big bunch. We were growing up then. Imagine we were 14, 15, 16. Some of the oldest parents in that class would be 18, 19. Maybe older. But we're young people. Have a son.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember what you used to do much in Sunday school? What? Do you remember what you used to do in Sunday school much?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, we just each, you know, the classes were all divided. You had to go in there and learn your verses and study the list. That was the sign. You know, you get those. You know. We had to study those and know our list by that time. It was a nice time. There was one, Sal, Silver Moore. We always had to quote a verse from the Bible and he always, his verse was Jesus wept and he would say that every time. And on Friday nights there was,

speaker: Anna Utt
words: um, I think it was, I think it was,

speaker: Anna Utt
words: oh, you know where everybody meets and they had plays and programs and...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Literary? It was?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, literary. I lost that word for a while. Yeah, and that was nice. That was fun.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What were they like?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I remember they had a master or a leader or something each time.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I just about forgot that. That was on Friday nights.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They had song?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Songs. People speak pieces or tell stories or... And it was always fun.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that just, those were in the schools, weren't they? Was it just the families whose kids who went to school there would come down?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, well, anybody.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It must have been everybody was welcome because I remember older people, I mean, you know, young people but they were too old, much too old for school.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the debates? Did they have debates?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, I don't. That was a long time ago.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Not right now. Sometimes you can think once in a while, you know, you can go back and think of things that happened then.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Some special program or something.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: If you think of anything like that, you should scribble it down and then when I come back, I don't know whether you'll remember. That would be great. Because not many people, most people have very hazy memories, literally, of specific things that would happen. They sound like they were pretty fun.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And then of course up there they had their ladies' aid and they had their sewing, quilting, all of that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go to that as a kid?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Maybe just a little bit.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would your mother go to that?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Sometimes, when she had mine. I think that's amazing. I think they're making more stuff there for one of the wars, you know, bandages or I don't know what you're into. But they have been, I don't know how that is.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did news of the war affect people around here? Did it mean much to them, the war, the Roam Roam?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: World War I, I can't remember when it was over with. And I remember folks worrying about it. But otherwise, you know, I can't remember the impression of that very much.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I would be 18 or 19. I would be 12 or 13. Then the 12 or 13 year old wasn't.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: You know you don't have TV to watch and you couldn't keep up on the news like you do today. And then the folks never, what it worked was to dwell on it. We always had enough to eat. So I guess that's all kids are interested in really.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did the girls stay more home than the boys did in your family?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, I left when it was time to go to high school. But Betty didn't graduate from high school. She had all the bad eyes at that time. And so she didn't go on. She took music lessons. And she kept up on her music beautifully. Now she teaches music lessons for the last 50 years or so.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What does she play?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: She has a nordic. She's good on that. She gives music lessons. Maybe 15 a week or something. That don't sound like much. Kids go to school, you know, and they have to come mornings and evenings.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You left home right when you graduated from eighth grade? Right after that?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, right afterward. And I went down to potlatch. The first one I worked for was Dr. Thompson. His wife. In the home.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I loved that place to work.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you expected to do very much?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, not there. I was kept busy in the morning. And then after I got my new dishes done, then I was expected to rest. Even though I didn't care for that. I didn't get about having to rest and have, you know. But then when the evening meal was time for that, I was asked to get up and go to work again.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When did you go to school?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I worked for them in the summer. Then when school started, I went to Elsie's. Just Elsie's. And stayed there on my board. That was a beautiful place to stay too. They were a favorite subject to us.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So you never lived at home again after eighth grade?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes, I did. We came home then in fall of 23 maybe. And then we walked from Princeton. Well, last time at school. Until I graduated.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I was home then.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: What, you told me a story last time about when your mother got sick at one point. And she wanted you to come home and quit high school.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it important to your folks for you to go to high school? For the kids to go to high school?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, it seemed to me. I know they tried awful hard to keep all of them in school. The boys, you know, then didn't care much about high school. They didn't think that that was all that necessary. They had been getting along a long time without going to high school. So they kind of walked on it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But your parents encouraged them to go?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yes.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: My dad got a Ford fine made. I let the boys take that to school, you know, so they wouldn't have to walk. That was quite a bit later after I graduated. But they somehow get up to the junior year and then they would quit, except the last two younger ones. They finished.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They don't know what to do with them yet?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The other student would be abundant, marked, flawed. I don't know. Yeah, I think that they all quit junior year.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were they anxious to get out and make the almighty dollar?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, they didn't care. That was the thing. It wasn't so hard. They had to find work, you know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: School doesn't mean so much. It didn't, I mean. Now, they not only have to have high school, they have to have college.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And that's nice. You get old fast enough anyway without, you know, starting to work when you're a kid.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did the girls in general, were they more interested in school than the kids? Was that true for more families than just yours?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think it was true, you know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It seemed like I can remember a lot of boys that didn't know, I mean, graduate. Okay.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well,

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: What would, because we talked about breakfast earlier.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was dinner like at home too?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: When you got home from school, or when we got home from school, sometimes it was stew or soup, you know. And mom would have all kinds of vegetables in it, you know, and then either rice or noodles or macaroni. You know, us could smell that now at the end of the woods. And then that didn't take us long to get home. From then on, you know, we'd have our big bowls and then knock two off crackers. But she always had home made bread. It smells good to me just now. That was just, it just seemed like that was the greatest time in my fellas life. You know, rushing home and tank up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have a whole wheat cereal lunch? Would you eat that much?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, mom would cook whole wheat lots of times.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that for breakfast?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, well, sometimes we'd have them for breakfast. You know, she would cook it during the day and then we'd have a bowl of it with sugar and milk before we went to bed. Or sometimes she'd have to leave it on the heater stove and it would be ready to get into it in the morning too. And I still like that.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That was a good way of doing it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, when we first moved from up there, he went back to Spain with the old farmers that he had farmed for, you know. And we were alone up there, mom and us kids. And then he got work on the section and then he got the boss of it, so he just, of the crew. So he just stayed there until he retired.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long was the day when he worked? How long did he be gone?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: He just had an eight hour day. In the wintertime it was different. He usually takes a train in Princeton and right up the track to the end of these lines. And then walked back down the road, peeling out switches, you know, when the snow was on, stuff like that. That was hard when he got open at all. Sometimes he'd pick the carton or anything.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, the carton.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: The carton. He always hard made it. But that was just a lot of kids.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: To feed the children.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you use to tan your hides at all? The beak hides or anything? Would you give them or sell them to someone or did they just get tossed in the hides?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I don't know. I can't remember as a little kid, but in life you never say so though.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father hunt much?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, he never did shoot again.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was he against shooting here?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, when the boys got older, the young boys got older, they used to hunt. But my dad, he didn't and I don't know why. He was just anti that way.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But he liked the me and the boys got it, I know. Maybe he can have a better life than that. And I know he did one time because Hawks used to bother the chiefs of that. And I shot one one time. He climbed upon a log and stood up there so smart and crazy. I got him but it knocked me down off the log. Back down on the ground. That wasn't funny.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How far out of Harvard did you live?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: A mile from Harvard. The store. The post office.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Which one?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: It would be North. I think it was where the old schoolhouse was and then just straight up three -quarters of a mile up this road. And it was never hard to walk to school or anything. Sometimes I'm bad because there's so much snow.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And the frost would freeze and make a crust. And that was when it was fun. Keep off our boots. We didn't have to wear them. And then we'd get to scoot down to school and start trotting at home. Because then we'd go down the flat. We didn't have to stick up the hilly road up there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go to dances? Did all the kids troop together?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think they had the one with the piano.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Ruby, again, played the piano. She was one of the ones that did. And then they had a violin. Maybe sometimes drums. And they had a dance on Friday night. Oh man, they were fun. But that took us.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would your mom stay at home?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Most of the time she did. He'd take us down and I didn't see that we'd get home. So they thought we was old enough to... And that was... He used to let us go with the boys then when we got older. The last times he'd show up to dance to see that we did it ourselves. I mean, you know, he didn't like to go to a dance and then run outdoors or anything else. We went to the dance and was supposed to stay in the building.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And oftentimes popped up around there to see that we did. Of course we didn't ever walk in either. To leave the hall. Because you'd hear people talking about others who did, so...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Seems like a long time ago.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But yet awfully short time too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were things stricter than you think?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I think that it was because maybe there was just times when there was something else.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Hi.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How are you?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Just fine. Good. Now this is my son's wife. My daughter -in -law. And this is Mrs. Sam.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And right now she's taking me. So turn her off.
